turns.text: Hello Carolyne, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. I guess… yeah, we can start. It’s funny, I’ve spent so much time reading about this, but actually talking about it feels a bit different. 

Being in this clinic in Somerville—it’s a mix of feelings. I feel like I’m constantly trying to prove I’m a "good" patient, you know? I walk in with my lists of questions, usually printed out or scribbled in my notes app, about progesterone levels or whether the weight gain is on track. I think I do that because if I focus on the data, I don’t have to focus on the fact that I’m sitting there alone. It’s hard to be twenty-three and see all these couples holding hands, talking about nursery colors, when I’m still just trying to figure out how to balance my job hunt with the fact that my body feels like it’s doing something completely outside of my control.

My midwife is kind, and she keeps telling me I’m "low risk," which I know is good, but it doesn't stop the anxiety. I’m still waiting to really *feel* the movement, that constant reassurance. Until then, it’s just this strange, quiet waiting game. I just want to feel like a mother, not just a biological project I’m managing. Does that make sense?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

Honestly, I think I built it up in my head to be this really scientific, precise exchange. I spent so much time tracking my LH surges and basal body temperature that I expected the appointments to feel like a continuation of that—very clinical, very data-driven. I pictured myself sitting down with someone who would walk me through the biology of what was happening, almost like a high-level briefing on fetal development. I wanted that, I think, because information felt like the only thing I could actually grip onto.

When I actually walked into the clinic in Somerville for the first time, it wasn't quite like that. It was louder, busier, and honestly, a bit more overwhelming than I anticipated. It’s a blur of fluorescent lights and other people’s conversations. I expected a partner-in-care dynamic, but the reality is that the time is so short. They move so quickly from the heart rate check to the next patient that I barely have time to breathe, let alone get through my list of questions. 

It felt… isolating, I guess? I didn't feel like the student-collaborator I imagined I’d be. I felt like another low-risk file on a desk. It’s hard to reconcile that professional, hurried environment with the massive, life-altering change happening inside me. It felt very sterile, even when everyone was perfectly nice.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation—a real, sit-down dialogue where someone would explain what the numbers meant in the context of my specific, little person. I imagined a midwife or a doctor leaning in, looking at the screen with me, and saying, "Look, here is why we track this, and here is how it affects the next few weeks." I wanted that intellectual bridge to feel more secure. 

The reality was almost entirely digital and hurried. I’d get a notification on the patient portal that my results were ready, and usually, it was just a string of clinical markers and abbreviations that I’d have to frantically Google while sitting at my kitchen table. If I did get a call, it was always the same: "Everything looks normal, you're low risk." 

"Normal" is a relief, of course, but it’s also a bit empty. It felt like I was being handed a piece of paper that confirmed the mechanics of a machine were working, but it didn't address the *human* side of the fear. There was no space to say, "The results are fine, but I'm still terrified because I can’t feel them move yet." I feel like I'm reading the manual for a car I don't know how to drive, and the people who know the roads are just waving me through the toll booth without checking if I'm actually okay to steer.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really hoped for a "person." You know, that idealized version of maternity care where you see the same face every single time, someone who would eventually learn my name without needing to glance at the chart first. I wanted someone who would recognize the tremor in my voice when I ask about heartbeat fluctuations, someone who would eventually just *know* that I’m doing this on my own and maybe offer a bit more than the standard medical checklist.

But the reality is definitely more of a rotation. I see whoever is on the schedule for that day at the clinic. It’s like a relay race where I’m the baton being passed from one provider to the next. I have to re-explain my anxieties every single time, or worse, I have to rely on them having read my notes—which they clearly don't have time to do thoroughly. 

It makes me feel so small. Every time I walk in and see a new face, I have to perform the "I’m a prepared, low-risk patient" routine all over again just to be taken seriously. There’s no continuity of trust. I’m not a person with a story to them; I’m just a booking in a slot. It’s incredibly lonely to have to keep re-introducing myself to the people responsible for my baby’s life.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Practically, it’s a nightmare because I have to manage my own medical record. I spend so much energy trying to summarize my last visit—the concerns I raised, the answers I got—that I feel like I’m doing their job for them. It’s exhausting to constantly be the one keeping the thread together when I’m already navigating the exhaustion of the second trimester and the stress of job hunting.

Emotionally? It’s the silence that gets me. When you don't have that consistent person, there’s no space to build a relationship where you can be vulnerable. I’ve stopped asking the questions that really matter—like how to handle the isolation or the fear that I’m doing something wrong—because I don’t want to be "that" patient who needs emotional support from a provider who’s already three minutes behind schedule. 

I’ve learned to put on a mask. I go in, I ask about the heartbeat, I get the "you're doing great," and I leave. It makes me feel like I’m just a vessel they’re inspecting, not a person going through the biggest, scariest, most beautiful thing in the world. I feel like if I had one person who knew me, maybe I wouldn’t feel like I’m drowning in the quiet parts of the day.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected it to be a bit more holistic, honestly. I thought the exams would be an opportunity to talk about how *I* was feeling—the fatigue, the strange shifting sensations, the way my body feels less and less like my own. I thought they would look at me as a whole person, acknowledging that this change is as much mental as it is physical.

Instead, the reality feels very fragmented. The exams are always focused on specific measurements: the fundal height, the blood pressure, the weight. It’s all about the metrics of the pregnancy, not the experience of being pregnant. When I’m on the table, it’s like my body is being audited. They check the boxes, they measure the growth, and then it’s, "Everything looks good, see you in two weeks." 

It’s dehumanizing in a way I didn't see coming. It’s clinical to the point of being cold. When they touch my stomach to check the baby's position, it’s purely functional—it’s not a moment of connection or recognition of what’s actually happening in there. I feel like a piece of equipment being calibrated. I leave those appointments feeling physically looked after, sure, but emotionally? I feel completely invisible, like my own discomfort and my own wonder aren't part of the protocol.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one time—I think it was around my 16th-week checkup—where a provider I’d never met before came in. They didn’t really introduce themselves, just sort of bustled in and immediately started with the fundal measurement. There was no "How are you feeling?" or "Is it okay if I move your shirt?" It was just, lift, measure, record. 

But then, they made a comment about my weight. It wasn't even a mean comment, just something like, "You're gaining right on target, which is good since we don't want any complications." It felt like such a cold, blunt way to talk about my body, especially since my body is already feeling like a stranger to me. I'm twenty-three, I’m navigating all these body image shifts, and suddenly my weight is just a data point for a graph they’re watching. 

I just sat there, my shirt pulled up, feeling incredibly small and exposed. I didn't say anything because I didn't want to seem like a "problem" patient, but I walked out to my car and just cried for ten minutes. It’s hard to articulate why that hit me so hard, but it felt like they were managing a biological process, not talking to a woman who is doing her best to grow a human in the middle of a really uncertain time in her life.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Thankfully, I haven't been diagnosed with anything like that yet. Since I'm labeled "low risk," I suppose I’m supposed to be grateful, but sometimes I wonder if that classification is exactly why I feel so unsupported. 

I’ve read *everything* about gestational diabetes and pre-eclampsia—I was honestly kind of obsessed with the symptoms before I got pregnant, because, like I said, I wanted to understand the "mechanics." I expected that if I *did* get a diagnosis, it would trigger a shift in care—that I’d suddenly have more access to specialized attention, more frequent monitoring, or at least someone who would sit me down and create a concrete plan. I imagined that being "high risk" would force the system to pay closer, more attentive, and more human-centered notice to me.

But watching the other women in the waiting room, I’m not so sure anymore. I see the ones who clearly have more complex needs, and they often look just as exhausted and hurried as I do. It makes me realize that even if something *did* go wrong, the system seems designed to manage the *condition*, not the person. If my "low risk" status means I’m just a number, I fear that a high-risk diagnosis would just turn me into a more complicated, more managed number. It’s a strange paradox, wanting to be taken seriously but dreading the cold, clinical way that would actually manifest.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I really thought that because of the sheer volume of "what to expect" books and apps, there would be a direct line of communication. I assumed that if I woke up in the middle of the night feeling panicked about a weird pain or just the silence of the baby, I’d be able to talk to someone who could actually talk me down. 

The reality is like hitting a brick wall. When I have a concern that isn’t a full-blown emergency, I’m funneled through a labyrinth of automated phone systems or generic patient portal messages that don't allow for nuance. If I call the office, I get a triage nurse—who is, I’m sure, incredibly busy—but it feels like I’m an inconvenience. They have these rigid protocols: "If X, then Y." If I don't fit perfectly into their flow chart, the conversation just stalls. 

It makes me feel like I’m constantly having to weigh my anxiety against the burden of bothering them. I’ve sat in my apartment in Somerville, staring at my phone, wondering if I should call about a concern, and then deciding against it because I can’t bear to be treated like I’m just an anxious, hysterical young girl. The system isn't responsive to *me*; it's only responsive to crises. If you’re not bleeding or in labor, you’re on your own.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Honestly, more times than I care to admit. There’s this constant internal negotiation—I’ll sit there for an hour, hyper-focused on my body, asking myself, "Is this a real symptom, or am I just being the young, inexperienced, over-anxious girl they probably roll their eyes about?" 

A couple of weeks ago, I had this strange, persistent cramping. It wasn't unbearable, but it was *there*, and it felt different. I spent the whole morning hovering over the phone. I kept thinking about the triage nurse who’d have to pick up. I could almost hear the sigh in their voice before they even answered, the implication that I was just another low-risk, first-time mom who didn't know what her body was supposed to feel like. I didn't want to be the girl who cried wolf or wasted their time when they could be helping someone who was actually in trouble.

So, I waited. I stayed in my apartment, tried to distract myself with my job applications, and just… sat in the panic. Eventually, the feeling passed, but the guilt and the fear stayed. It’s a terrible feeling to realize that my fear of being judged by the system is actually stronger than my need for medical reassurance. I shouldn't have to be a "good, low-maintenance patient" to deserve peace of mind, but that’s exactly how it feels.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I had this vision that being pregnant would be this massive, communal event. In the movies, the friends are all there for the gender reveal, or they’re throwing these elaborate showers. But the reality is that at twenty-three, most of my friends are just… elsewhere. They’re starting their careers, dealing with student loans, or moving to new cities. I feel like I’m in a different life stage entirely, and it creates this strange, quiet distance. 

I think I expected my friends to be more "in it" with me, but how do you ask a friend who’s worried about a job interview to come over and sit with you while you’re anxious about a heartbeat scan? It feels like asking too much. And my family… they’re supportive in their own way, but they don't quite get the isolation of the city. They keep telling me how "special" this is, which is true, but it doesn't help when I’m sitting in my apartment in Somerville feeling overwhelmed by the weight of doing this without a partner.

It’s just lonely. I have this circle of people who love me, but there’s no one who actually *witnesses* the daily reality of it—the late-night Googling, the constant, low-level hum of anxiety, the feeling of my body shifting. I’m the only one who really knows what’s going on inside me, and that’s a heavy thing to carry alone.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I guess I expected a lot of advice—like, a constant stream of "do this" and "don't do that" from my parents or older cousins. In a way, I got that, and it’s actually been a bit suffocating. They see the pregnancy as a miracle, which it is, but that lens makes it hard to talk about the messier stuff—like how scary it is to be twenty-three and wondering if I’ll ever land a stable job again, or the fact that I’m just terrified of the actual birth process.

What I didn't expect was the silence from some of my closest friends. I thought they’d want to come over and touch my stomach, or ask about the appointments, but instead, it’s like they don’t quite know how to handle it. The conversation gets awkward, and then it’s like we’re back to talking about their dating lives or their grad school drama, and I’m just… there. I think they’re scared of the "mother" word, or they just don’t know how to relate to someone who’s suddenly in such a different world. 

It’s strange. I have people around me, but I feel like I’m standing on the other side of a glass wall. The support that didn't materialize was the kind that simply says, "I see you, this is hard, and I'm here even if we don't talk about the baby." I needed a partner, or at least a witness, and I ended up with a lot of people cheering from the bleachers while I’m down on the field by myself.

I think the hardest part is that I’m trying to be this perfect, modern, independent woman—the one who researches every single development milestone and keeps her prenatal care organized—but inside, I’m just twenty-three and I’m terrified. 

I’ve spent so much time worrying about whether I’m "doing pregnancy right" that I’ve forgotten how to just *be* pregnant. I look in the mirror and I see my body changing, and I catch myself trying to analyze it like a clinical specimen, looking for the "scientific" reason for why I feel so vulnerable. But you can't logic your way out of the fear that comes with a silent, empty room at night. 

I keep wondering: what happens when I'm actually in labor? Will the same cold, efficient, "low-risk" logic apply then? Will I still be just a file to be processed, or will there finally be someone who looks at me and sees a mother who is doing the bravest thing she’s ever done? I want to stop being a project to be managed. I want to feel like a person who is capable of bringing another person into the world. I’m just trying to bridge that gap between the data I consume and the life I’m actually living. It’s the hardest thing I’ve ever done, and I think I’m realizing that no amount of research could have prepared me for how lonely it feels to be the only one who truly knows what this feels like.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, absolutely. It happens all the time, honestly. It’s usually this subtle, dismissive way people talk to me because I’m twenty-three and I’m doing this on my own. My mother is the biggest offender—she means well, she really does, but she views my pregnancy through this lens of her own experiences from thirty years ago. I’ll mention something my midwife suggested, like a specific nutritional change or a way to handle morning sickness, and my mother will immediately chime in with, "Oh, don't listen to them, just drink ginger ale like I did." 

It feels like my own research, my own agency, is just a suggestion to them. They see me as a "young girl having a baby" rather than a woman making deliberate, informed choices about her prenatal care. 

Then, there’s the clinic. I remember asking a nurse if I could opt out of a certain routine check because I was feeling really anxious and just wanted a moment to breathe. The way she looked at me—it wasn't mean, but it was *firm*—was like she was talking to a child who didn't know what was good for her. "We have to do this, honey, it’s policy." It was like my boundaries just didn't exist against their "protocol." It's incredibly frustrating. I’m the one carrying this life, I’m the one doing the work every single day, yet my own intuition is treated like it’s just a symptom of my age or my "low-risk" anxiety. It makes me feel like I’m losing control of my own body, piece by piece.

I think the biggest takeaway for me, if I’m being honest, is that "low-risk" is a really lonely label. You want to feel lucky, right? You want to hear that everything is fine and just go about your day. But that label becomes a shield for the providers, too—it gives them a reason to keep things at arm's length. Because I’m not a "problem" case, I don't get the extra time, the deeper conversations, or the reassurance I crave. I’m just a box to be checked.

Sometimes I think about the birth—about that moment in the hospital—and I get this cold sweat. I’m terrified that I’m going to be in that bed, in the most vulnerable moment of my life, and I’m going to be treated like an assembly line project. Will they listen to my preferences? Will they see me as a person who has spent months researching and preparing, or will they just see a twenty-three-year-old girl who they need to "manage" through to the finish line?

I want to be more than just my biological markers. I want someone to look at me—really look at me—and acknowledge that becoming a mother is a monumental, terrifying, and beautiful transition. I don’t want to be a patient in a system; I want to be a partner in my own journey. And right now? I feel like I’m just a tourist passing through a very cold, very efficient hospital, waiting for the day where I finally get to step off the track and actually *be* a mom. I just hope I don't lose myself entirely before I get there.

It’s heavy, thinking about that moment of birth. I keep playing it out in my head like a film scene. Will I have someone there who knows *me*—not just my blood pressure or my weight, but the person who’s been spending all these quiet nights in Somerville trying to build a foundation for her child? Or will it be a revolving door of residents and nurses who see my age and my "low-risk" chart and just assume I don't know what I'm doing, or worse, that I don't have a say in what happens to my own body?

I’m terrified that the system will drown out my voice at the exact moment I need it the most. You know, I’ve spent so much energy trying to be the "good patient"—the one who’s polite, the one who does her homework, the one who doesn't complain about the long waits or the lack of continuity. But I’m starting to wonder if that "goodness" is exactly what’s keeping me invisible. 

If I could go back to that first appointment, I’d tell myself to stop apologizing for taking up space. I’d tell myself that my intuition, the way I feel about my own body, is more important than any line on a spreadsheet. I just want to feel like, when the time comes, I’m the primary person in the room—not a spectator in my own labor. I’m just trying to keep my head above water until I can finally, truly step into that mother role. It’s hard to find that strength when everyone treats you like you’re just a temporary guest in a clinical process. I just want to be seen.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, yes. It feels like every time I walk into the exam room, my body is treated like public property. It’s not just the weight—it’s the judgment behind it. I’ve had providers, mostly ones I’ve only seen for five minutes, make offhand remarks like, "You’re doing well with the weight gain, we don't want you getting too comfortable and letting it get out of hand." 

It lands like a physical weight on my chest. I’m already navigating these massive, confusing changes—my clothes don't fit, my center of gravity is off, and I feel like an alien in my own skin—and then they reduce it all to a statistic. When they talk about my diet, it’s always in that patronizing, "you-better-be-careful" tone, as if I’m not already stressing about every single thing I put into my mouth because I’m so worried about the baby’s development. 

It makes me feel like I’m failing a test I didn't even know I was taking. It’s supposed to be "support," right? But it feels like policing. I want someone to ask me *why* I’m struggling to eat or how I feel about these changes, not just to look at a chart and make a cutting remark. It’s exhausting, constantly having to defend the way my body is choosing to grow. It makes me want to shrink back, to hide away, rather than be open about the struggles I’m actually having. They see a "low-risk" pregnancy, but they don’t see the woman who’s feeling less like a person every time they comment on her shape.

It’s hard to shake the feeling that I’m being graded on a curve I didn’t choose. Every comment about my diet or my weight—or even just the way they look at my chart and say, "everything is normal"—makes me feel like I’m being analyzed for errors. And if I feel like I’m constantly being judged for how I’m growing, how am I supposed to trust them when the time comes for the big decisions during labor?

I look back at the beginning, when I was tracking my basal body temperature and LH surges, and I realize now that I was trying to master the biology so I wouldn’t feel so exposed. I thought if I had the knowledge, I’d be safe. But I’m realizing that the "science" doesn’t make the experience any less isolating. I’m sitting in these offices, surrounded by people who have the degrees and the charts, but they don't seem to have the time for the *human* part of the equation.

I’m really looking forward to—and dreading, in a way—that final transition. I just want to reach the end of this and know that the person I am, the mother I am, matters more than the patient file in their computer. I don’t want to be a "successful case study" for a clinic in Somerville; I want to be a woman who navigated this, with all the fear and the loneliness and the questions, and came out the other side as a mother. I just hope that when I finally get there, someone is actually listening.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I turn to the internet, honestly. It’s my default, almost like a reflex now. Before I even pick up the phone to call the clinic, I’m scouring forums—Reddit, baby apps, even those old-school message boards. I think I turn there because those spaces feel a little more honest. You see other women talking about the exact same things I’m feeling—the isolation, the weird aches, the way the system dismisses your fears—and it’s the only place where I feel like I’m not just a "low-risk" file. 

I also have a couple of friends who are a bit further along or have already had their babies, but it’s complicated. I don't want to be the "needy one" or the one who’s always complaining. So, I mostly just lurk. I read their stories, their birth experiences, and I try to piece together the reality behind the medical jargon. It’s like being a detective in my own pregnancy.

It’s sad, isn't it? I have a medical team that I’m supposed to trust, yet I feel more understood by a screen full of strangers who have never met me. But in those forums, I’m not just a number on a chart. I’m a person navigating a really scary, quiet transition, and that validation—even from someone I’ll never meet—is sometimes the only thing that keeps me from spiraling. I wish I could get that same level of humanity from my own appointments.

It’s strange how much I’ve curated this digital circle of people who don’t even know my real name. We share the most intimate details—the fear of a silent Doppler, the panic when you haven't felt a kick in a few hours, the way you feel like your body is a rented space—and there’s no judgment there. It’s the only place where I feel like I’m actually *learning* what being a mother means, beyond just the clinical milestones. 

But then I have to log off and go to my appointment at the clinic, and I have to switch back to this "compliant, low-risk" persona. It feels like I’m living in two different worlds. In one, I’m a person with fears and intuition; in the other, I’m a file to be processed. I’ve started to realize that the most important information I’ve gathered hasn't come from my midwife’s charts or the hospital pamphlets. It’s come from these women who are doing the same thing I’m doing—trying to survive the quiet, the loneliness, and the fear of the unknown. 

I just keep hoping that by the time I’m actually in that delivery room, I won’t have to hide who I am anymore. I want to be able to say, "I am scared," or "I don't understand this," without being told it’s just the anxiety of a first-time mother. I want my knowledge—the stuff I’ve learned from the science of it, yes, but also the stuff I’ve learned from the *feeling* of it—to be respected. I’m just trying to make it to the other side. I hope that’s enough.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I wish I could say there were many times it felt like a partnership, but honestly? It’s almost always me being informed of a plan. The appointments have this rhythm—a very brisk, pre-determined cadence—that doesn’t really leave room for "What do you think, Carolyne?" 

It’s usually, "We’re going to do this test," or "We’re checking for this now," and I’m just expected to nod along. It’s like I’m a passenger in a car where someone else is driving, and they’ve already mapped out the entire route without asking if I’m comfortable with the speed or the direction. When I do try to interject—like when I ask about a certain procedure's necessity or if there's an alternative—I’m often met with this polite but firm, "Well, this is standard protocol for a pregnancy like yours." 

That "standard protocol" phrase is probably the thing I hate the most. It effectively ends the conversation. It implies that my individual needs don’t matter because the system has already decided what’s "best" for a low-risk patient. There was one time, during a routine scan, where I asked if we could take an extra moment to look at something specific, and the tech just sighed and said, "We’re already behind schedule." It wasn't even a medical decision; it was just a matter of convenience for them. 

I don't think they realize how much that stings. It’s my body, my baby, and my life-altering experience, but in that clinic, I’m just an obstacle to their efficiency. I’m not a partner; I’m a project to be completed as quickly as possible.

I think the most frustrating part of that "passenger" feeling is that I’m actually *prepared* to be a partner. I’ve done the reading, I understand the basic science, and I’m ready to engage. But there’s no invitation to the table. When I try to open a dialogue about my birth preferences or even just how I’m managing the physical symptoms, it’s like I’m speaking a language they don't have time to translate.

Sometimes I wonder if it’s my age. Do they see twenty-three and automatically think, "She doesn't really know what she’s asking for"? It’s patronizing in this quiet, insidious way. I’m not asking for a radical alternative plan; I’m just asking for a conversation that includes my voice. I want someone to look at my chart, sure, but then I want them to look *at me* and ask if this plan actually aligns with who I am and what I’m scared of. 

The silence between appointments is where I do all my real work—the thinking, the worrying, the planning—but none of that ever makes it into the exam room. It’s like there’s this massive, invisible wall between my inner world as a mother and the clinical world of my pregnancy care. I just wish I didn't feel like I had to choose between being a "good, compliant patient" and being the person who actually *knows* what’s best for her own baby. It shouldn't have to be a choice. I’m just trying to keep my head up until I can finally step into that role of mothering, where I’m not just a data point, but the one calling the shots. I’m so ready to stop being a "case" and finally start being a mom.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was one moment that really stuck with me—a routine ultrasound where I was just so desperate to see her moving, to have that tangible proof she was okay. The technician was moving the wand over my stomach so fast, barely pausing, just clicking away to get the measurements they needed for their report. I asked, almost as a whisper, "Is she okay? Does everything look normal?" 

The technician didn't even look up from the screen. She just kept typing and said, "I’m not allowed to tell you anything, you’ll have to wait for the doctor to review this." 

It was like the air left the room. I wasn't asking for a medical diagnosis or some complicated analysis; I just wanted to see a human face look at me and say, "Yes, she’s doing just fine." That blank, bureaucratic wall—the "I’m not allowed"—it made me feel like I wasn't a mother, but a stranger peering into a laboratory experiment. In that instant, my trust just evaporated. It wasn't that they were being malicious, but their complete inability to see the *human* fear in front of them, even when it was so obvious, was staggering.

I realized then that if I needed empathy or even just basic acknowledgement of my anxiety, I wasn't going to find it within that clinic’s walls. That was the moment I stopped viewing them as my partners. They were just technicians of a process I had to endure. I stopped asking questions after that. I just learned to wait for the impersonal email notification in my patient portal. It’s a sad way to go through something this monumental—feeling like you’re constantly being shut out of your own story.

It’s like I’ve had to build a version of myself that can survive that silence. After that ultrasound, I think I started closing off. I stopped bringing my printed lists of questions because I realized that even if I asked them, the answer was always the same: "wait for the portal," "it’s standard protocol," or "everything is normal." 

I feel like a ghost in my own prenatal care. I walk in, they check the baby's heartbeat, they update the chart, and then I’m dismissed. They have their schedule, their metrics, and their safety guidelines—all of which are important, I guess—but they don't have a place for the person who is actually carrying the baby. It’s like they’re building a house, and I’m just the foundation they’re pouring concrete over. 

I’m starting to wonder what kind of mother I’ll be when this is all over. Will I still be this "anxious, low-risk, first-time mom" they keep referring to? I want to be able to trust my own voice again. I want to know that when I hold her, I’m the authority on her, not the clinic portal. I think I’m just trying to make it through the next few months without losing the sense of who I am—and who I’m becoming—in the middle of all this sterile, efficient, lonely procedure. I’m so ready to just be a mom.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

It’s almost every single time, if I’m honest. Every time I sit in those hard plastic chairs in the waiting room, I’m surrounded by couples. They’re leaning into each other, whispering, looking at ultrasound photos together. Even if they aren't saying anything, there’s this shared weight that they’re carrying—that, or someone to hold their hand when the Doppler is silent for those extra few seconds while the midwife hunts for the heartbeat.

When I’m in there alone, it’s not just that I lack the emotional support; it’s that I lack an advocate. When a provider says something in that brisk, clinical shorthand, I have to process it, verify it, and decide how to respond all in the same breath. If I had someone there, they could hold the space while I try to figure out what just happened. They could be the one to ask the follow-up question I’m too intimidated to voice. 

Being alone makes me feel like I’m constantly on the defensive. I feel like I have to be "on" the entire time, projecting this image of the capable, single, twenty-three-year-old who doesn't need anyone. It’s exhausting. There’s a specific kind of hollowness when I walk out of the clinic, clutching my little packet of information, and I have to get into my car by myself. No one to debrief with, no one to share the relief that the heartbeat was steady. It’s just me and the silence, which is exactly what I’m trying to get away from. It reinforces that I’m the only one truly witnessing this, which makes the whole thing feel much more precarious than it probably is.

I think the hardest part is that when you’re there by yourself, there’s no one to witness the *you* that exists outside of the clinic walls. The providers only ever see the version of me that’s trying to stay composed, trying to ask the "right" questions so I don't look like an uninformed, scared girl. They never see me when I’m actually struggling, when I’m overwhelmed by the sheer scale of what I’m doing. They see the file, not the person.

I feel like a spectator of my own pregnancy. I watch other people come in as a unit—a team—and I realize that the system is built for that. It’s built for two people to be processing the information, two people to be holding the stress, two people to be excited. When you’re solo, the system just doesn't know what to do with you. You become an outlier. They treat you like you’re "low risk," which is medically accurate, but emotionally, it feels like I’m navigating a marathon while everyone else has a teammate to hand them water.

I’m really trying to find my footing, to believe that I’m enough on my own. But it’s a constant battle against the feeling that I’m an anomaly, or that I’m somehow not fulfilling the "picture-perfect" experience. I just want to reach the end of this and know that I didn't just endure it, but that I actually *owned* it. I’m just trying to make it there in one piece.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

It happens after every single appointment. I leave the clinic feeling like I’ve just been handed a glossy, one-page summary of a textbook, and I walk straight to my car—or sometimes just walk a few blocks into the square—and start searching.

It’s never about the basic biology; I can find the phases of fetal growth in any app. I’m looking for the *context*. I’m looking for someone to explain why my midwife told me to "not worry" about a specific symptom when I can find ten other women who had the same thing and were told it was a precursor to something else. I’m looking for the nuance that the "low-risk" label washes away. 

Specifically, I find myself searching for what the actual birth experience is like at my specific hospital. I want to know: *Do they actually listen to your preferences? Do they try to force you onto the monitor the whole time?* I’m looking for the "insider" information because I don’t trust the official line. When the providers tell me everything is "standard protocol," I go online to see if that protocol is actually for my benefit, or if it’s just for the clinic’s efficiency. I’m looking for a map of the hospital culture, I guess—trying to figure out how to navigate a system that I already know doesn't see me as a person. I want to know how to advocate for myself when I’m at my most vulnerable. I’m looking for an instruction manual on how to be heard.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the main thing is that I’m still waiting for someone—*anyone*—in that clinic to look at me and acknowledge that I’m doing this. Not just "doing" the pregnancy, but doing the hard work of building a life for this baby, alone, while trying to build my own future. 

It feels like they think they’re being kind by treating me like a low-risk, standard-issue patient. They think they’re keeping things simple for me. But it actually feels like they’re erasing the most significant, difficult part of my life. I don't want to be "protected" from the reality of my own situation by being treated like a number. I want them to see the twenty-three-year-old in the chair, the one who’s balancing the anxiety of the Doppler checks with the stress of rent and a job hunt. 

When I walk into that room, I feel like I’m in a costume—playing the part of the compliant patient. I just wish I could show up as the person I actually am, with all the fear and the strength that entails. I’m not just a vessel for a fetus; I’m the person who has to raise this child. I just hope that by the time I’m in labor, I don’t feel like I have to keep performing that role. I hope I can finally just be me. That’s all I’m really asking for—to be seen.
